title: Anger At Billionaires: Useful Or Not
dek: When you read about wealth hoarding, your jaw tightens and your gut aches. This isn't a personal failing; it's a biological response to systemic injustice, and your body knows exactly what it's doing.
bodyMarkdown:
You sit down with your phone, the blue light flickering against the darkened room. It’s just past midnight, maybe 3 AM, the kind of hour when the world shifts into a quieter, more vulnerable frequency. You scroll through headlines about billionaires expanding their empires, wealth stacking like bricks in a wall that feels impenetrable. The names are familiar - those who seem to hoard riches while the rest of us navigate narrowing streets and shrinking opportunities. As you read, your jaw tightens, the masseter muscles clench with an almost ritualistic insistence. It’s like your body is trying to hold in a storm, the teeth pressing together against a tide of frustration that threatens to spill out. The coldness pools in your gut, not quite nausea but a hollow, sharp ache that wraps around your psoas, making your hips want to fold inward against the weight of injustice.


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:42.965Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

This isn’t some fleeting irritation. It’s a full-body response, one that wakes you in the dead hours, when the world’s quiet makes your own internal noise impossible to ignore. The trapezius muscles along your neck and shoulders stiffen, pulling your head forward as if bracing against an invisible blow. You might find your breath shallow, the diaphragm constricted, as though the very act of breathing demands more effort than usual. This is not pathology, this is biology. The body remembers what the mind sometimes tries to push away - the jaw remembers the betrayal of equity, the gut knows the threat of imbalance. 

You are not failing because you feel this anger. This is not a personal shortcoming or a sign of weakness. It is your body’s ancient response to perceived threat and injustice. Anger is a signal, a flare sent up by your nervous system to alert you to harm, to inequality, to a system that grinds many down while a few rise. It’s a primal marker that something is off, that the scales are tipped dangerously, and it refuses to be silenced by polite conversation or the social scripts that tell you to keep quiet, to smile, to not let your fury show. The jaw remembers centuries of women taught their rage was ugly, dangerous, something to be swallowed. But the body knows better. It knows anger is a messenger, not a monster.

In Traditional Chinese Medicine, teachers like Giovanni Maciocia have long described how emotions are stored and expressed through the body’s organs and meridians. Anger, in particular, is linked to the Liver, an organ that moves Qi and ensures the smooth flow of energy. When you read about wealth hoarding, about systems designed to protect the few at the expense of the many, your Liver Qi can stagnate. That stagnation feels like tightness, a knot in the side of your ribs or a heaviness pressing down through your trapezius and levator scapulae. This is not abstract. It is felt. The body translates social injustice into physical sensation because the body knows - it cannot separate the political from the personal. Your nervous system lights up, the sympathetic branch kicking in to prepare you for action or protest, even if your societal role asks you to remain still.

The anger at billionaires, then, is not just righteous indignation; it is a biological imperative. Your system is calling you to pay attention, to respond in some way that restores balance - whether through speech, movement, or finding some ritual of release. This is why, when the news plays on the endless loop of wealth disparity, you feel your body tighten, your breath catch, and your sleep fracture. This is your body’s way of saying, “This matters. This is not okay.” And it is your right to listen, to honor that signal, and to find ways to move that energy through you rather than letting it calcify into resignation or despair.

The rage is not a flaw; it is a fuel. It is a connection to the collective reckoning that every generation faces when the structures that govern us fail to serve the whole. In the silent prayers of your diaphragm, in the clenched rebellion of your masseter, your body is speaking truths that words alone can’t carry. This article is a conversation with that body, an invitation to explore whether this anger is useful or not - not to dismiss it, but to understand it deeply, to sit with its weight and its wisdom. Because the body knows. And the jaw remembers.

The body knows what it’s been asked to carry, even when the mind has been told to shut the hell up. Gabor Maté’s work reminds us that the chronic stress sewn into the seams of society - inequality, invisibility, the grinding weight of systemic injustice - doesn’t just live in headlines or in our nervous systems. It roots itself deep into the body’s tissues, the very cells that make us who we are. When you swallow your fury at billionaires, when you tuck that fire away because polite society brands your anger as ‘toxic’ or ‘unproductive,’ you are not just repressing an emotion. You are asking your liver, your fascia, your diaphragm to hold the fallout of that repression. This is not pathology, this is biology.

Imagine the liver, that dense, reddish-brown organ nestled under your right ribs, as an alchemist of the body’s poisons and emotions. In Traditional Chinese Medicine, the liver houses anger; it’s the organ tasked with the smooth flow of qi, the body’s vital energy. When you suppress your rage, the liver’s energy stagnates, and this stagnation becomes physical. Your trapezius tightens, the levator scapulae knots up, and the fascia - the connective tissue that wraps muscles and organs like a wet suit - grows rigid, sticky with held tension. The diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs, clamps down, making your breath shallow and your anxiety deeper. Over time, this chronic tension does not simply dissipate; it calcifies into the body’s architecture. You begin to smell it in your skin, a faint metallic tang as your masseter clenches unconsciously, your jaw remembers each slight, each dismissal, grinding the history into bone.

Maté’s insights pull no punches: societal inequality is a chronic trauma, and trauma lives in the body. When the wealth gap widens, when billionaires accumulate fortunes built on the backs of unseen labor and systemic exploitation, that’s not just a political issue - it’s a biological one. Your liver, overloaded with the toxins of chronic rage and the biochemistry of stress, begins to falter. The fascia hardens, no longer supple but brittle and resistant, a physical echo of the emotional and social rigidity that’s been imposed upon you. You’re not broken; you are responding to a relentless environment that demands you hide your fury to survive.

This is why tending to the liver with serious, grounded herbal allies is not some new-age fluff but an act of profound self-respect and survival. Milk Thistle, with its active compound silymarin, is a stalwart guardian of hepatic health. It supports the liver’s ability to regenerate and detoxify, helping to clear the biochemical residue left behind by suppressed anger and systemic stress. Dandelion root, a bitter that stimulates bile flow and gently encourages the liver to release what no longer serves, is another ally you can trust. When you take a tincture of [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20), you’re not just ingesting a plant; you’re inviting the liver to open pathways, to unclench the body’s fascia, to begin the slow, stubborn process of healing from within.

The body’s response to suppressed anger is neither mysterious nor shameful. It is a language of survival, and the herbs are translators. They help the liver do its job, clearing out the biochemical muck left by the trauma of silence and the weight of inequity. You can feel it in your body - the loosening of the jaw, the release of the psoas that’s been holding your fight-or-flight like a secret, the softening of the diaphragm so your breath can finally deepen beyond the shallow, guarded sips you’ve been taking. This is not about erasing anger or pretending it’s gone; it’s about acknowledging what the body has been asked to carry and giving it the tools to carry less.

Remember, the liver is not an isolated organ but a nexus of your lived experience, a biological archive of your emotional history and your environment. When you care for it with herbs like Milk Thistle and Dandelion root, you’re doing more than detoxing - you’re honoring the truth that your rage is valid, your body’s response is real, and your healing is possible. This is a rebellion against the narrative that tells you anger is ugly or dangerous. Instead, you are learning that anger, when met with respect and care, becomes a medicine in itself - one that pulses through your fascia, breath, and blood, urging you to reclaim what society tried to silence.

When you feel your fury flare at the sight of billionaires hoarding unimaginable wealth, it’s not just a political reaction or a passing frustration - it’s your nervous system sounding an alarm that reverberates through the collective body. Resmaa Menakem, in his work on somatic abolitionism, teaches us that trauma is not just an individual burden but a shared wound lodged deep within the muscle, fascia, and sinew of our communities. The body remembers systems of oppression the way the jaw remembers clenched rage, long after the mind tries to move on. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your anger at resource hoarding is the body’s way of registering an ecological threat to the collective nervous system, an ancestral recognition that the uneven distribution of life’s essentials - food, water, shelter, and dignity - fractures the very fabric of our interconnectedness.

In the quiet hours just before dawn, when the world is still draped in its dark velvet, your psoas muscle may tighten in the hollows of your low back and hips, a visceral echo of the helplessness encoded in your cells. This deep core muscle, the ancient seat of fight-or-flight, is where your body stores the tension of systemic injustice. The diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs, constricts as if to choke off your breath, mirroring the suffocation you feel witnessing this grotesque accumulation of wealth while bodies around you go unmet, unseen. The levator scapulae, nestled between your neck and shoulders, stiffens as you carry the weight of systemic rage. This is not some abstract indignation; it’s a somatic symphony of survival instincts pulsing through your anatomy.

Menakem’s somatic abolitionism calls us to listen to these bodily signals not just as individual sensations but as communal transmissions. When you recognize that your anger is a collective signal, you begin to understand that healing requires moving beyond intellectual debates into the realm of embodied action. The body knows, and the body must move. This is why the act of releasing the psoas or opening the diaphragm is not merely physical - it is political, a reclamation of your life force that has been cornered by systems designed to fragment and isolate.

Here is a practice to help you move this specific type of systemic, helpless anger through your body. Find a quiet space where you won’t be interrupted. Lie on your back on a firm surface, your knees bent and feet flat on the floor. Place one hand on your lower belly and the other on your chest. Begin by noticing the way your diaphragm moves as you inhale and exhale, the subtle rise and fall beneath your hands. As you breathe, imagine the anger pooling in your psoas, that deep muscle anchoring your hips to your spine. Allow yourself to send your breath directly to that area. On your inhale, draw breath into the belly, expanding gently but fully, imagining the diaphragm dropping down, creating space. On the exhale, engage your lower belly, feeling the psoas relax as you slowly press the back of your knees toward the floor, a subtle activation that invites release without strain.

This movement awakens the psoas without forcing it. It’s a conversation, not an interrogation. You’ll notice a tremble or a sudden softening, the body’s way of unspooling trauma held tight in the muscle. Don’t push beyond what feels tolerable - that’s the difference between liberation and retraumatization. After several cycles of this breath and gentle psoas engagement, shift your awareness to the trapezius and levator scapulae. Roll your shoulders slowly backward and downward, as if drawing the weight of billions off your neck, letting the muscles unwind. The release may be small, but it’s a defiant act. Each unwinding is a stitch in the collective healing cloth, a refusal to carry the load of oppression silently.

Remember that this work is not about erasing anger but about moving it through your body so it doesn’t calcify into bitterness or despair. The body remembers, yes - but it also has the capacity to transform. By attending to the psoas and diaphragm, you’re doing somatic abolitionism in practice: dissolving the muscle memory of helplessness, reclaiming your breath, and reconnecting with the collective pulse of resistance. Your fury is not ugly; it’s a vital message from the depths of your embodied self, a call to remember that you belong to a living, breathing community that deserves justice and care.

You sit with that anger, raw and unfiltered, and your body remembers what it has always known but you might have tried to forget: this fury is not something to shove down your throat or apologize for. It is a messenger, a sharp-edged clarifier that cuts through the fog of social niceties and polite silences. When you unclench your masseter and feel the levator scapulae loosen around your neck, you create the space to listen. The anger tells you where your boundaries have been overstepped, where your needs have been ignored, where the world’s greed has piled upon your shoulders like a weight too heavy for the diaphragm to lift. This is not pathology, this is biology. The body knows what it needs to protect itself, and fury is one of those ancient signals.

You do not need to convert this anger into something shiny and acceptable to the marketplace of emotions. You do not need to dress it up as the fuel for manifesting better things or turning it into a positive vibe. Instead, you let it be what it is: a fierce boundary marker, a herald of injustice, a call to arms for community care. When you speak your anger aloud, the words come out heavy, grounded in the somatic truth of your experience. You might say, “I will not take this on,” or “This is not my burden alone.” The trapezius might tense in readiness, but you are steady, because you know your limits and you honor them. You are not responsible for fixing the world’s inequities alone, but you can claim your part in shaping a world where care is shared and boundaries honored.

This is where community care breathes in. The night deepens and the air around you smells faintly of crushed mugwort and cedar smoke, a reminder that the earth holds memory and wisdom. You gather your people, those who also carry this fire, those whose psoas tighten not from anxiety but from righteous rage. Together, you create a container where this anger is witnessed, held, and transformed into collective strength. This is not about ‘rising above’ or silencing the noise; it is about letting the body’s wisdom guide you to mutual support. You share herbal teas brewed with skullcap or passionflower to soothe the nervous system after a session of speaking hard truths. You acknowledge the sharpness of your own responses, the quickened heartbeat, the heat behind your eyes, and you find ways to settle the body that do not mean erasing the anger but honoring it.

Your jaw remembers every time it clenched in frustration or grit its teeth through a moment of rage. This is not your shame; this is your signal. The herbs you choose - perhaps a tincture of black cohosh to ease tension, or a cup of chamomile steeped long to calm the diaphragm’s spasms - are allies in your somatic work, not a replacement for the truth you carry. You hold the fire, you do not snuff it out. You let it fuel your clarity, your ability to say no, your refusal to be gaslit by a world that profits from your silence.

You are not alone in this. Teachers like Robin Rose Bennett remind us that anger lives in the body as much as the mind, and that healing is about listening deeply to what the body demands. Herbs are tools, somatic practice is medicine, and community is the hearth where this fury burns without consuming you. This is how anger becomes a force for boundary-setting and communal resilience. Not a performance, not a polite request, but a grounded, somatic knowing that you are worthy of respect and care.

**Q:** How do I handle the rage I feel toward the ultra-rich who hoard wealth while so many suffer?  
**A:** Your rage is a natural response to systemic injustice, not a personal failing. The body stores this anger in the masseter and trapezius - you might notice jaw clenching or neck tension when you think about these inequalities. Allow yourself to express this fury in safe spaces - whether through movement, vocalization, or writing - without judgment. Use herbs like dandelion root to support liver detoxification, which in Traditional Chinese Medicine is linked to the smooth flow of anger, helping you process it without becoming overwhelmed. Remember, this rage can fuel boundary-setting and community action, but it does not need to become a crushing burden.

**Q:** What somatic signs indicate I am suppressing my anger, and how can I begin to release it?  
**A:** Notice if your diaphragm feels tight, if your shoulders hunch forward, or if your psoas muscles lock up when you’re stressed - these are classic signs of anger held in the body. The jaw often clenches unconsciously, and the levator scapulae might knot up around the neck. Begin by tuning into these sensations without trying to fix them instantly. Gentle movement, breathwork focused on expanding the diaphragm, or resting with warmth on the trapezius can help. Herbal allies like valerian root or motherwort can ease muscular tension and support nervous system regulation as you explore these somatic signals.

**Q:** Are there specific herbs that can help me honor my anger without dulling its edge?  
**A:** Absolutely. Herbs like skullcap and passionflower offer calming effects without suppressing the vital energy of anger. They soothe the nervous system and relax the muscles - especially around the neck and jaw - allowing you to hold your fury without it becoming destructive. Black cohosh can ease tension in the body while also supporting emotional balance. These herbs don’t erase your anger; they help you embody it fully and set boundaries from a place of strength, not exhaustion.

**Q:** How can I use my anger to build community care rather than isolation?  
**A:** Anger can isolate if you carry it alone, but when shared with trusted others, it becomes a connective force. Find or create spaces where your fury is acknowledged and honored as a collective experience - whether through women’s circles, activist groups, or herbal workshops. These gatherings allow your psoas to relax and your breath to deepen, signaling safety in numbers. Together, you can translate individual rage into communal boundaries and care practices, supporting each other’s healing and resistance. The body knows the difference between fighting alone and fighting with allies; lean into that knowing.

---

<section data-author="byline">
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality.

In our work on anger at billionaires: useful or not, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
